metadata.title: Alternative Dispute Resolution Research Landscape from 1981 to 2022

abstract: Alternative dispute resolution (ADR) is an important means of resolving disputes outside of traditional legal frameworks. It is usually adopted because of its flexibility, cost-effectiveness, and ability to preserve relationships that a contentious court battle might damage. This study aims to evaluate the scientific publication related to ADR. To do so, we used metadata of ADR-related articles from 1981 to 2022 collected in the Web of Science Core Collection and carried out a bibliometric, network, and latent Dirichlet allocation analysis. Our results indicate that ADR research is concentrated in North America, with research organizations from the United States accounting for most publications. At the same time, recent years have seen a shift to a more diverse group of countries, with the Chinese City University of Hong Kong and the Australian Victoria University leading in the last five years. The five main topics in ADR research include online dispute resolution for consumer protection, mediation for family law, arbitration in medical malpractice cases, ADR in construction projects, and ADR in employment law. The most frequent research areas assigned to ADR publications are Government & Law and Business & Economics. Network results show keywords ADR, Mediation, and Arbitration as central nodes, while the Chinese and North American organizations established the most frequent collaborations, addressing ADR applications in various sectors. The findings underscore the interdisciplinary nature of ADR research, its adaptability across industries, and the importance of cross-cultural research partnerships.

paragraphs: Alternative dispute resolution (ADR) is a term that encompasses various methods of resolving disputes without resorting to litigation (Balzer and Schneider 2021;Charkoudian et al. 2019;Strazisar 2018;Teague et al. 2020). ADR methods give Extended author information available on the last page of the article parties more control (Beebeejaun and Faccia 2022;Cortés 2022;Dauer 2002), flexibility (Balzer and Schneider 2021;Ibrahim et al. 2022;Olapade et al. 2019), confidentiality (Beebeejaun and Faccia 2022;Riveros and Coester-Waltjen 2019;Sustek and Holcapek 2017), and lower costs (Bradford et al. 2022;Illankoon et al. 2022;Kisi et al. 2020) than traditional court proceedings. The most common ADR methods are mediation and arbitration (Bradford et al. 2022;Kass and Rose 2016), but other methods may be associated with ADR, such as conciliation, expert appraisal, adjudication, and hybrid combinations of mediation and arbitration (Noone and Ojelabi 2020;Saeb et al. 2018). These methods differ in terms of the role and authority of the third party who assists the parties in reaching an agreement or a decision (Whaley 2022;Williams et al. 2020). ADR is commonly used in various contexts, including commercial disputes (Cortés 2022;Kisi et al. 2020), labor and employment disputes (Teague et al. 2020), family law disputes (Riveros and Coester-Waltjen 2019;Whaley 2022), and personal injury disputes (Lau 2022).

However, ADR methods have limitations and challenges that may hinder their wide use and effectiveness. For example, ADR may lack procedural safeguards (Riveros and Coester-Waltjen 2019), neutrality (Illankoon et al. 2022;Noone and Ojelabi 2020), and accountability (Menkel-Meadow 2015) that may be guaranteed by litigation. Furthermore, some parties may resist or abuse ADR methods for strategic reasons or due to power imbalances (Menkel-Meadow 2015).

Previous studies have explored various aspects of ADR, including its methods (Charkoudian et al. 2019;Cortés 2022;Menkel-Meadow 2015;Saeb et al. 2018), applications (Cheung et al. 2002;Dauer 2002;Hann et al. 2019;Peters 2021;Qin 2022;Sustek and Holcapek 2017), and challenges (Deutsch et al. 2018;Ibrahim et al. 2022;Illankoon et al. 2022). Other studies have a more specific nature, focusing on the use of ADR to, e.g., healthcare (Balcerzak and Leonhardt 2008), construction (Cheung et al. 2002), and family law (Hodson 2008;Minter 2018). Although ADR is important in many aspects of life, as far as we know, a few studies have attempted to take stock of the scientific literature related to this subject. They are review articles that explore its history and evolution in specific countries (McManus and Silverstein 2011;Peters 2021;Wang and Chen 2020) or make broad assessments of its use (Strazisar 2018). While informative and comprehensive, these articles lack a systematic and quantitative analysis of the global ADR research landscape.

To address this gap, our study aims to evaluate the scientific publication related to ADR. To do so, we used metadata of peer-reviewed articles related to ADR indexed in the Web of Science Core Collection (WoS) from 1981 to 2022 and combined Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA), bibliometrics, and network analysis to analyze these publications. LDA is a topic modeling method that combines natural language processing (NLP) and machine learning (ML) techniques to uncover themes from unstructured text datasets (Si 2022). Its main purpose is to discover latent topics in large amounts of unstructured text data based on word frequencies (Hagg et al. 2022;Newman et al. 2014). It has been used with bibliometric techniques and network analysis as a form of content analysis (Chen et al. 2022;Si 2022;Song et al. 2022;van Dijk et al. 2023). Bibliometrics uses statistics to quantitatively analyze articles' metadata (Ninkov et al. 2022). For its part, network analysis uses statistics, 1 3
Alternative Dispute Resolution Research Landscape from 1981… mathematics, and graph theory to generate visualizations from co-occurrences (Chen et al. 2019).

Our study builds upon the existing literature by comprehensively analyzing the ADR research landscape of over 40 years. Additionally, to the best of our knowledge, this is the first study to present such analysis using ADR-related scientific publications. By identifying the main research topics and research areas and the leading organizations and countries producing knowledge on ADR, we hope our study may provide valuable insights for researchers, practitioners, and policymakers, helping to guide future research directions, foster interdisciplinary collaboration, and ultimately contribute to the continued development of ADR methods and practices.

sections.header_raw: Database, Search Strategies, Data Collection, and Selection
sections.header_norm: database search strategies data collection and selection
paragraphs: We analyzed scientific publications related to ADR indexed in WoS, considering all available years up to 2022 . The search strategies use two descriptors: alternative dispute resolution and out-of-court settlement. Only articles and review articles were included (from now on referred to as articles). Table 1 details the search strategies. All searches and collection of articles records were carried out on 2023-03-16. Set #1 searches in articles' titles, and set #2 searches in articles' titles, abstracts, and keywords. While set #1 is contained in #2, set #3 excludes from set #2 the records present in #1.

The records of sets #1 and #3 were imported into the data/text mining software VantagePoint 11.0 for treatment and analysis, comprising two distinct datasets (datasets #1 and #3). All records in dataset #1 were automatically included in the analysis. In turn, the records of dataset #3 were submitted to an automatized screening process to exclude articles not related to the scope of the study. This step was necessary because there are two types of keywords in WoS search results: author keywords and Keywords Plus. The latter is extracted from the articles' cited references, potentially bringing "garbage" to the analysis. To remove eventual records included only

Table 1 Search strategies performed in PubMed, Scopus, and WoS Set Search strategies Results #3 #2 NOT #1 686 #2 TS=("alternative dispute resolution*" OR "out-of-court settlement*") Refined by Document Types: Article or Review Article Timespan: 1945-01-01 to 2022-12-31 (Publication Date) 905 #1 TI=("alternative dispute resolution*" OR "out-of-court settlement*") Refined by Document Types: Article or Review Article Timespan: 1945-01-01 to 2022-12-31 (Publication Date)

because one or more search strategy descriptors occurred in the Keywords Plus, we proceeded as follows. First, we cleaned and standardized the list of author keywords using VantagePoint's list cleanup tool (and the matching ruleset General) along with manual cleaning. Thus, variations of the search strategy's descriptors were standardized as alternative dispute resolution and out-of-court settlement. Then we merged the lists of Abstracts and cleaned author keywords. Next, we searched the merged list for the records containing alternative dispute resolution or out-of-court settlement and their variations, which were selected for analysis. As a result, the number of records was reduced from 641 to 597. Thus, excluding 44 records that were included in the dataset only because they appeared in the Keywords Plus. Finally, we fused dataset #1 and treated dataset #3 using VantagePoint's dataset fusion tool.
The final dataset contains 816 records-219 from dataset #1 and 597 from dataset #3.

paragraphs: To generate the LDA topics, we used in-house software written in Python. The software uses the Python libraries 'spaCy' and 'Gensim.' The 'spaCy' library is targeted at NLP tasks, while 'Gensim' provides tools for unsupervised topic modeling. The former was used for word tokenization, selection, and exclusion of stop words, and the latter for topic modeling. A list of stop words provided by 'spaCY' with adjectives, adverbs, conjunctions, interjections, nouns, prepositions, and pronouns was automatically excluded. We also excluded words from the initial query (dispute, resolution, alternative, court, and settlement) and related to structured abstracts (introduction, background, material, method, methodology, result, finding, conclusion, and highlight). After testing and qualitatively evaluating the model's content with different topics (four to eight), we chose the model with five topics. For qualitative studies that involve interpreting topic content, relying on human judgment is preferable over statistical methods for determining the optimal number of topics (Garechana et al. 2019;Hagg et al. 2022). The frequencies of the main words of each topic are available in the Supplementary Material.

sections.paper_id: Mota et al. - 2023 - Alternative Dispute Resolution Research Landscape from 1981 to 2022
paragraphs: To perform the analysis, data were collected from the fields of publication years, source title (abbreviated), author keywords, research area, countries, and author affiliations (organization and city and country). The research area is a WoS subject classification scheme that covers about 250 subject areas from all knowledge fields (Clarivate: incites.help.clarivate.com/Content/Research-Areas/wos-researchareas.htm). Author affiliations and author keywords data are subject to variations and typographical errors. Therefore, combining and analyzing these fields requires cleaning and standardization. We performed this task using VantagePoint's list cleanup tool (and the matching ruleset General) and manual cleaning. In cleaning and standardizing the list of author Keywords, which covers 59.93% (489) of the articles, we grouped related descriptors at a more general level. For example, the 1 3
Alternative Dispute Resolution Research Landscape from 1981…

descriptors court mediation, judicial mediation, institutional mediation, mediation ethics, etc., were grouped as mediation. This more aggregated level allows us to simplify the presentation of the results without prejudice of content specificities since we can identify the original descriptors and the articles they are assigned to. After this process, the number of author Keywords was reduced from 1.637 to 902, and the number of author affiliations (85.78% coverage; 700 articles) from 841 to 778. The network analysis assesses the last five years of publications (2018-2022), encompassing 373 articles (45.71% of the total). This is because we opted to analyze recent co-occurrences between author keywords and research areas and the recent flow of knowledge and collaborations between research organizations and countries. By analyzing recent data, our objective is to capture the latest trends, collaborations, and knowledge flows in the field of ADR. This approach is frequently employed in network analysis to identify trends that may not be visible over longer periods of time (Camacho et al. 2020;Wasserman and Faust 1994). Using VantagePoint, we created co-occurrence matrices of author keywords, research areas, research organizations, and countries. The matrices were imported into the network analysis software Gephi 0.10, where we built the networks and generated the network's metrics. In the networks, the nodes are the author keywords, research areas, research organizations, or countries, and the edges are the relationships between them (in terms of co-occurrences). The nodes' sizes and colors and the edges' thickness of all networks were given by their weighted degree centrality (WDC) -the sum of the weights of all edges linked to a node (Braga and Mota 2021). In addition to WDC, we used closeness centrality (CC), eigenvector centrality (EC), and betweenness centrality (BC) to analyze the nodes' relationships within the networks of research organizations and countries. CC, EC, and BC measure, respectively, the proximity of a node to all other nodes, the links a node has with central nodes, and the shortest path a node takes to connect to others in a network (Braga and Mota 2021). The network's layout was given by the force-directed layout algorithm Fruchterman-Reingold (Gephi: https:// github. com/ gephi/ gephi/ wiki/ Fruch terman-Reing old). The results of all network metrics calculated in Gephi are available in the Supplementary material.
sections.text:
To perform the analysis, data were collected from the fields of publication years, source title (abbreviated), author keywords, research area, countries, and author affiliations (organization and city and country). The research area is a WoS subject classification scheme that covers about 250 subject areas from all knowledge fields (Clarivate: incites.help.clarivate.com/Content/Research-Areas/wos-researchareas.htm). Author affiliations and author keywords data are subject to variations and typographical errors. Therefore, combining and analyzing these fields requires cleaning and standardization. We performed this task using VantagePoint's list cleanup tool (and the matching ruleset General) and manual cleaning. In cleaning and standardizing the list of author Keywords, which covers 59.93% (489) of the articles, we grouped related descriptors at a more general level. For example, the 1 3

Alternative Dispute Resolution Research Landscape from 1981…

descriptors court mediation, judicial mediation, institutional mediation, mediation ethics, etc., were grouped as mediation. This more aggregated level allows us to simplify the presentation of the results without prejudice of content specificities since we can identify the original descriptors and the articles they are assigned to. After this process, the number of author Keywords was reduced from 1.637 to 902, and the number of author affiliations (85.78% coverage; 700 articles) from 841 to 778. The network analysis assesses the last five years of publications (2018-2022), encompassing 373 articles (45.71% of the total). This is because we opted to analyze recent co-occurrences between author keywords and research areas and the recent flow of knowledge and collaborations between research organizations and countries. By analyzing recent data, our objective is to capture the latest trends, collaborations, and knowledge flows in the field of ADR. This approach is frequently employed in network analysis to identify trends that may not be visible over longer periods of time (Camacho et al. 2020;Wasserman and Faust 1994). Using VantagePoint, we created co-occurrence matrices of author keywords, research areas, research organizations, and countries. The matrices were imported into the network analysis software Gephi 0.10, where we built the networks and generated the network's metrics. In the networks, the nodes are the author keywords, research areas, research organizations, or countries, and the edges are the relationships between them (in terms of co-occurrences). The nodes' sizes and colors and the edges' thickness of all networks were given by their weighted degree centrality (WDC) -the sum of the weights of all edges linked to a node (Braga and Mota 2021). In addition to WDC, we used closeness centrality (CC), eigenvector centrality (EC), and betweenness centrality (BC) to analyze the nodes' relationships within the networks of research organizations and countries. CC, EC, and BC measure, respectively, the proximity of a node to all other nodes, the links a node has with central nodes, and the shortest path a node takes to connect to others in a network (Braga and Mota 2021). The network's layout was given by the force-directed layout algorithm Fruchterman-Reingold (Gephi: https:// github. com/ gephi/ gephi/ wiki/ Fruch terman-Reing old). The results of all network metrics calculated in Gephi are available in the Supplementary material.

paragraphs: This study presents some limitations. First, the analysis relies solely on publications indexed in the Web of Science Core Collection, which may not capture all relevant research on ADR. Second, the search strategy excludes articles that do not use the terms "alternative dispute resolution" or "out-of-court settlement" in their titles, abstracts, or keywords. However, this was the authors' choice to avoid including articles not focusing on ADR. Also, the network analysis only assesses the last five years of the period. While this choice might exclude some important earlier works, it highlights recent trends and research collaboration between organizations. In any case, network analysis is usually applied to assess short periods of time to identify recent collaborations or flows of knowledge (Camacho et al. 2020;Wasserman and Faust 1994).

paragraphs: The most frequent words and the titles of the five LDA topics are presented in Table 2. The word frequencies for the topic and all publications are provided in the supplementary material. The title of Topic 1 is "Online Dispute Resolution (ODR) for Consumer Protection." It includes the words "consumer," "ODR," "online," "technology," "commerce," and "protection" and relates to the use of ODR for settling consumer disputes. The second topic is labeled "Mediation as an ADR Model in Family Law" and is mainly characterized by the words "mediation," "agreement," "family," "mediator," and "child." This topic concerns using mediation as an ADR for resolving family disputes, such as divorce and child custody. Topic 3 ("Arbitration and ADR in Medical Malpractice Cases") includes words such as "patient," "medical," "malpractice," "physician," "surgery," "payment," and "error." This topic is linked to the use of ADR in cases of medical error and its implications for patients and physicians. Titled "ADR in Construction Projects," topic 4 is described by the words "conflict," "mediation," "construction," "public," "international," and "development." This topic is linked to the use of ADR in construction industry disputes, possibly involving public infrastructure and international construction projects. Topic 5 contains the terms "employee," "employment," "right," "employer," "workplace," "arbitration," and "union." The title is "ADR in Employment Law" and refers to using ADR in employment disputes to ensure the rights and justice for both employees and employers.

sections.paper_id: Mota et al. - 2023 - Alternative Dispute Resolution Research Landscape from 1981 to 2022
Mota et al. - 2023 - Alternative Dispute Resolution Research Landscape from 1981 to 2022
paragraphs: The oldest article related to ADR was published in 1981 in Sociologie du Travail and proposed to analyze litigation alternatives (Abel 1981). From 1981 to 2017, an annual average of 12.49 articles was published, peaking in 2012 (26 articles). From 2017 to 2018, a 358% growth marked a significant change in the level of annual publications. From 2018 to 2022, an average of 74.6 articles were published annually, peaking in 2022 (81 articles). This path led to the publication of 45.71% of all articles in the last five years of a 40-year time series (Fig. 1a). From 1981 to 2022, the two most frequent journals publishing ADR-related articles were Negotiation Journal (2.08%) (Negotiation J: onlinelibrary.wiley.com/journal/15,719,979) and Journal of Legal Affairs and Dispute Resolution in Engineering and Construction (1.72%) (J Leg Aff Dispute Re: ascelibrary.org/journal/jladah) (Fig. 1b). As the main descriptor of the search strategies (Table 1), ADR is expected to be the most frequent author keyword. It is present in 272 articles, followed by Mediation (120) (Fig. 1c). In 48.04% of all articles, Government & Law is the most frequent research area assigned to the articles, followed by Business & Economics (19.48%) (Fig. 1d).

paragraphs: Alternative Dispute Resolution Research Landscape from 1981… Of the 25 organizations that published five or more articles between 1981 and 2022, 56.00% are North American (Fig. 2a), including the University of California, the ranking leader with 2.21% of all articles. The only Chinese organization among the top 25 ranks second (1.84%). Researchers affiliated with North American organizations published 29.04% of all articles, followed by researchers affiliated with the United Kingdom (UK) (9.31%) and Australian (7.23%) organizations.

paragraphs: As mentioned, the period 2018-2022 covers 45.71% (373) of all articles. The three most frequent keywords in the last five years were ADR, Mediation, and Arbitration (Fig. 3a). They are present in 46.65%, 24.40%, and 13.94% of the articles, respectively. In the network of keywords (Fig. 3b), these three keywords are also central nodes according to their weighted degree centrality. They rank first (WDC: 750.0), second (WDC: 452.0), and fourth (WDC: 218.0), respectively. In turn, the three most frequent research areas were Government & Law (57.91%), Business & Economics (9.65%), and Social Sciences-Other Topics (6.17%) (Fig. 3c). Considering the co-occurrence of research areas, Government & Law (WDC: 46.0) and Family Studies (WDC: 28.0) are the two most central nodes in the network. Business & Economics ranked third (WDC: 14.0), and Social Sciences-Other Topics sixth (WDC: 8.0) (Fig. 3d). Ten articles were assigned at the same time by WoS to the research areas Government & Law and Family Studies, comprising the principal co-occurrence between research areas in the last five years. The subjects of these publications range from online dispute resolution (ODR) (Schmitz and Wing 2021; Wasser 2021), to legal education (Nussbaum 2022; Pruett et al. 2018), and family issues (Capdevila Brophy et al. 2020; Deutsch et al. 2018; DeVries 2018; Minter 2018; Morhe 2022; Schmitz and Wing 2021; Wasser 2021) such as domestic violence (DeVries 2018; Morhe 2022) and divorce (Wasser 2021).

With six (1.61%) articles each, the Chinese City University of Hong Kong and the Australian Victoria University were the most frequent research organizations publishing on ADR in the last five years (Fig. 4a). The City University of Hong Kong was also the most central node in the network of organizations (WDC: 6.0), together with the New Zealand University of Auckland (WDC: 6.0). Victoria University ranked fourth (WDC: 2.0) (Fig. 4b). With three articles published together, researchers from the City University of Hong Kong and the University of Auckland 1 3 Alternative Dispute Resolution Research Landscape from 1981…

established the most frequent institutional collaboration of the period. Their joint articles assess the intention to use ADR in construction projects (Lee et al. 2018a(Lee et al. , b, 2021)). With 15.55% of all articles, the USA was the leading country in publications related to ADR (Fig. 4c) and the most central node of the network of countries (WDC: 158.0) (Fig. 4d), followed by Australia (10.46%; WDC: 92.0), and the UK (9.65%; WDC: 90.0). With four joint publications, researchers from Chinese and North American organizations established the most frequent collaborations. sections.paper_id: Mota et al. - 2023 - Alternative Dispute Resolution Research Landscape from 1981 to 2022
paragraphs: The five LDA-generated topics in ADR research illustrate the main areas where ADR is applied. For example, articles related to ODR for consumer protection indicate that this is an important research area with practical implications for consumer rights (Abedi and Ahmad Yusoff 2011). ODR is a branch of ADR that uses digital technology to facilitate the resolution of disputes between parties (Akhtar et al. 2022;Mania 2015). With the rapid expansion of e-commerce and the increasing reliance on technology, using ODR in consumer protection has become more critical than ever (Mania 2015;Peters 2021). ODR can facilitate swift and cost-effective resolutions for consumer disputes, promoting fairness and trust in digital transactions (Abedi et al. 2019b;Akhtar et al. 2022). Similarly, the results demonstrate the growing recognition of mediation as a preferred ADR method in family law disputes. Mediation can provide a less adversarial and more collaborative approach to resolving conflicts, prioritizing the well-being of children and preserving familial relationships (Hodson 2008;Riveros and Coester-Waltjen 2019). It can also help in estate and trust disputes by reducing the grief of losing a family member and dealing with family resentments (Whaley 2022).

Additionally, the prevalence of arbitration and ADR in medical malpractice cases underscores their potential for mitigating litigation costs (Balcerzak and Leonhardt 2008;Dauer 2002;Kass and Rose 2016;Sohn and Bal 2012;Sustek and Holcapek 2017;Wang et al. 2020a), reducing delays (Dauer 2002), and fostering a more open dialogue between patients and physicians (Kass and Rose 2016;Sohn and Bal 2012;Szmania et al. 2008;Wang et al. 2020a). These mechanisms may also improve patient safety and medical practice standards (Szmania et al. 2008;Thomas 2000;Wang et al. 2020a). Similarly, the use of ADR in construction projects, particularly in public infrastructure and international projects, highlights its ability to address complex and high-stakes disputes efficiently (Cheung et al. 2002(Cheung et al. , 2019;;Illankoon et al. 2022;Kisi et al. 2020;Lee et al. 2018b). ADR can offer flexible, tailored solutions that cater to the unique nature of construction disputes, minimizing disruptions and fostering better working relationships among stakeholders (Cheung et al. 2002;Illankoon et al. 2022;Kisi et al. 2020). As for ADR in employment law, the results indicate its potential to balance the interests of employees and employers (Wiryawan and Bunga 2020) and promote a harmonious workplace environment (DeSouza 1998;Fox and Stallworth 2009;Russell 2021).

The significant increase in ADR-related publications in recent years suggests that there is a growing interest in using ADR as a means of resolving disputes. This could reflect a recognition of the benefits of ADR over traditional litigation, such as its cost-effectiveness (Bradford et al. 2022;Illankoon et al. 2022;Kisi et al. 2020), flexibility (Balzer and Schneider 2021;Ibrahim et al. 2022;Olapade et al. 2019), and the possibility to give more control to the involved parties (Dauer 2002;Beebeejaun and Faccia 2022;Cortés 2022). It could also reflect a desire to find more efficient and effective ways of resolving disputes as the legal system becomes increasingly overburdened (Imam 2020;Jain et al. 2021). The two most frequent journals publishing ADR-related articles, Negotiation Journal and Journal of Legal Affairs and Dispute Resolution in Engineering and Construction, emphasize the interdisciplinary nature of ADR research, spanning negotiation studies, legal affairs, and engineering and construction domains. Both journals are devoted to different aspects associated with ADR, from negotiation instruction to dispute avoidance and resolution (Journal of Legal Affairs and Dispute Resolution in Engineering and Construction 2023;Negotiation Journal 2023). This reinforces that ADR mechanisms are versatile and adaptable to various industries and contexts.

The most frequent author keywords-ADR, Mediation, and Arbitration-underscore the primary focus of ADR on these two dispute resolution methods. While ADR is an umbrella term that encompasses various methods of resolving disputes outside the traditional court system, mediation and arbitration are actual methods of ADR (Zeller and Trakman 2019). Mediation is a voluntary, informal, and confidential ADR process in which an impartial third party, the mediator, helps disputing parties communicate and negotiate with each other. The mediator does not impose a decision on the parties but rather facilitates a constructive dialogue between them (Menkel-Meadow 2015; Sgubini et al. 2004). On the other hand, arbitration is a more formal ADR method where an impartial third party, the arbitrator (or a panel of arbitrators), listens to the arguments and evidence presented by the disputing parties and then makes a decision. The decision, called an award, is generally final and legally binding on the parties. The arbitration process is more structured than mediation, with specific procedural rules and timelines, but still less formal and more flexible than traditional court litigation (Menkel-Meadow 2015; Sgubini et al. 2004). Additionally, other methods can even combine mediation and arbitration (often called MedArb or Arb-Med) (Ross and Conlon 2000;Stipanowich 2020). The prevalence of these terms in recent years suggests a continued emphasis on exploring and refining these core ADR methods.

The results also show that Government & Law was the most frequent research area in ADR literature, which suggests that legal frameworks play an important role in shaping the use of ADR. A supportive legal framework can help to facilitate the use of ADR and ensure that it is integrated effectively into the legal system (Wang and Chen 2020), while a restrictive legal framework can hinder the adoption and effectiveness of ADR (Barrett and Barrett 2004). The presence of Business & Economics as the second most frequent research areas underscore the significant role ADR plays not only in the public sphere but also in the private one-such as the case of private dispute resolution arrangements established by employers and unions in Ireland (Roche 2022). The co-occurrence of Government & Law and Family Studies in the last five years visualized in a network (Fig. 3d) demonstrates an increased interest in applying ADR mechanisms to family-related disputes, such as divorce (Wasser 2021) anddomestic violence (DeVries 2018;Morhe 2022). This finding aligns with the earlier discussion on the growing importance of mediation in family law.

Compared to the full period , the evolution of publications over the last five years (2018-2022) suggests a shift from a more American-centered production of knowledge to a more diverse group of non-American organizations leading 1 3

Alternative Dispute Resolution Research Landscape from 1981… the ranking of the most productive ones. The Chinese City University of Hong Kong and the Australian Victoria University emerged as the most frequent research organizations publishing on ADR in the last five years, highlighting their contributions to advancing the field. While the first one had most of its publications related to the construction sector (Cheung et al. 2019;Lee et al. 2018a, b), the latter had contributions to ODR (Abedi et al. 2019a, b). As previously mentioned, ADR is particularly important for the construction sector due to the complex nature of construction projects, which often involve multiple stakeholders, tight deadlines, and large budgets. The construction industry is prone to disputes arising from various factors such as contract interpretation, the scope of work, design issues, time extensions, and cost overruns (Illankoon et al. 2022;Kisi et al. 2020;Saeb et al. 2018). The same is true for ODR, which can play an important role in e-commerce and security in online environments by providing a convenient, efficient, and secure means to resolve disputes that arise from online transactions (Akhtar et al. 2022;Mania 2015;Wasser 2021).

As for the organization networks, the City University of Hong Kong and the University of Auckland formed the most frequent institutional collaboration. Again, the City University of Hong Kong dedicated most of its publications to ADR use in construction projects (Lee et al. 2018a(Lee et al. , b, 2021)). This collaboration emphasizes the importance of ADR in the construction industry, a topic identified in the earlier LDA analysis and the main focus of one of the journals with the most publications on ADR, the Journal of Legal Affairs and Dispute Resolution in Engineering and Construction.

Although organizations from several countries were among the leaders in ADRrelated publications in the last five years, the USA still has more organizations with five or more articles. The USA is also the most central node of the network of countries, establishing the most number of collaborations and relevant collaborations with other countries, playing a relevant role in connecting organizations from other countries-including Australia, and the UK, which are also central nodes in the network. These countries share a common law legal system, which has historically been more conducive to adopting and integrating ADR methods, such as mediation and arbitration, into their legal frameworks (Barrett and Barrett 2004;McManus and Silverstein 2011). Despite that, the most frequent collaborations of American authors were with researchers from Chinese organizations. Their joint publications addressed ADR applications such as resolving disputes between air carriers and passenger franchising (Qin 2022), international trade (Shang and Shen 2020), and medical disputes (Wang et al. 2020a). These collaborations indicate the potential for ADR to address a wide range of disputes across different sectors and the value of cross-cultural research partnerships in advancing ADR knowledge and practice.

Finally, it is worth mentioning that the expressive number of Chinese ADR publications may derive from the long history of mediation as a traditional method for dispute resolution in the country. While some sources assert that private mediation was employed as early as the Xia period (2070-1600 BC), others propose that written records documenting mediation as a dispute resolution method date back to the Western Zhou period (1100-771 BC). More recently, mediation was encouraged and practiced in communist-controlled regions during the Chinese civil war in the 1900s, and the People's Mediation Committees were among the first regulations implemented by the newly established government of the People's Republic of China (Wang and Chen 2020). Also, part of the publications from China come from institutions in Hong Kong, which under the principle of 'one country, two systems,' has a legal system different from that of Mainland China and is based on the common law (The government of the Hong Kong Special Administrative Region 2023).

The construction industry is prone to disputes arising from various factors such as contract interpretation, the scope of work, design issues, time extensions, and cost overruns (Illankoon et al. 2022;Kisi et al. 2020;Saeb et al. 2018). The same is true for ODR, which can play an important role in e-commerce and security in online environments by providing a convenient, efficient, and secure means to resolve disputes that arise from online transactions (Akhtar et al. 2022;Mania 2015;Wasser 2021).

As for the organization networks, the City University of Hong Kong and the University of Auckland formed the most frequent institutional collaboration. Again, the City University of Hong Kong dedicated most of its publications to ADR use in construction projects (Lee et al. 2018a(Lee et al. , b, 2021)). This collaboration emphasizes the importance of ADR in the construction industry, a topic identified in the earlier LDA analysis and the main focus of one of the journals with the most publications on ADR, the Journal of Legal Affairs and Dispute Resolution in Engineering and Construction.

Although organizations from several countries were among the leaders in ADRrelated publications in the last five years, the USA still has more organizations with five or more articles. The USA is also the most central node of the network of countries, establishing the most number of collaborations and relevant collaborations with other countries, playing a relevant role in connecting organizations from other countries-including Australia, and the UK, which are also central nodes in the network. These countries share a common law legal system, which has historically been more conducive to adopting and integrating ADR methods, such as mediation and arbitration, into their legal frameworks (Barrett and Barrett 2004;McManus and Silverstein 2011). Despite that, the most frequent collaborations of American authors were with researchers from Chinese organizations. Their joint publications addressed ADR applications such as resolving disputes between air carriers and passenger franchising (Qin 2022), international trade (Shang and Shen 2020), and medical disputes (Wang et al. 2020a). These collaborations indicate the potential for ADR to address a wide range of disputes across different sectors and the value of cross-cultural research partnerships in advancing ADR knowledge and practice.

Finally, it is worth mentioning that the expressive number of Chinese ADR publications may derive from the long history of mediation as a traditional method for dispute resolution in the country. While some sources assert that private mediation was employed as early as the Xia period (2070-1600 BC), others propose that written records documenting mediation as a dispute resolution method date back to the Western Zhou period (1100-771 BC). More recently, mediation was encouraged and practiced in communist-controlled regions during the Chinese civil war in the 1900s, and the People's Mediation Committees were among the first regulations implemented by the newly established government of the People's Republic of China (Wang and Chen 2020). Also, part of the publications from China come from institutions in Hong Kong, which under the principle of 'one country, two systems,' has a legal system different from that of Mainland China and is based on the common law (The government of the Hong Kong Special Administrative Region 2023).
sections.preview_head: The five LDA-generated topics in ADR research illustrate the main areas where ADR is applied. For example, articles related to ODR for consumer protection indicate that this is an important research area with practical implications for consumer rights (Abedi and Ahmad Yusoff 2011). ODR is a branch of ADR that uses digital technology to facilitate the resolution of disputes between parties (Akhtar et al. 2022;Mania 2015). With the rapid expansion of e-commerce and the increasing reliance on technology, using ODR in consumer protection has become more critical than ever (Mania 2015;Peters 2021).

sections.paper_id: Mota et al. - 2023 - Alternative Dispute Resolution Research Landscape from 1981 to 2022
paragraphs: This study provided an overview of the global research landscape related to ADR. The analysis of ADR research suggests a growing interest in applying ADR methods across various sectors and industries, such as ODR for consumer protection, mediation in family law, arbitration in medical malpractice and construction disputes, and ADR in employment law. Also, the increasing number of ADR-related may indicate recognition of ADR's advantages over traditional litigation, including cost-effectiveness, flexibility, and control for involved parties. The interdisciplinary nature of ADR research was evident in the wide range of research areas and journals publishing ADR-related articles.

The focus on mediation and arbitration as core ADR methods and the significant role of legal frameworks in shaping ADR usage underlines the importance of integrating ADR effectively into legal systems. The shift in research institutions publishing on ADR, from predominantly American universities to a more diverse group, and the international collaborations among countries reveal the expanding global interest in ADR and the potential for cross-cultural partnerships to advance ADR knowledge and practice. Overall, the results of this analysis indicate that ADR continues to be an important and evolving field of research, with significant practical implications for various industries and sectors, offering more efficient and effective ways of resolving disputes in an increasingly complex world.

Our research carries practical implications for ADR practitioners. The identification of major research themes provides practitioners with an overview of the evolving landscape of ADR, enabling them to stay informed about emerging trends and key areas of focus. The increasing diversity of geographical contributions and the prominence of cross-cultural research partnerships underscore the importance of understanding cultural nuances in ADR practice. Understanding these trends may also guide practitioners in identifying potential areas for collaboration and knowledge exchange. Lastly, the shift towards ODR illuminates the growing role of digital platforms in dispute resolution, underscoring the need for practitioners to gain fluency in these platforms.
